architecture.summary: This is an empirical study paper with no novel system architecture. The paper evaluates existing LLMs (GPT-3.5-turbo, Claude-3-Haiku, Gemini-1.5-Flash, LLaMA-3-8B, Gemma-2-9B) under three progressively constrained generation regimes: constrained decoding (JSON-mode), format-restricting instructions (FRI), and NL-to-Format conversion. Performance is measured across reasoning and classification tasks with outputs compared using both regex and LLM-based answer extraction.

biblio.title: Let Me Speak Freely? A Study on the Impact of Format Restrictions on Performance of Large Language Models

claims.dataset: GSM8K, Last Letter Concatenation, Shuffled Objects
claims.statement: Significant decline in LLMs' reasoning abilities under format restrictions

claims.dataset: GSM8K, Last Letter Concatenation, Shuffled Objects
claims.statement: Stricter format constraints generally lead to greater performance degradation in reasoning tasks
JSON-mode performs significantly worse than FRI on Last Letter task for GPT-3.5-Turbo, with 100% of responses placing the answer key before the reason key

claims.dataset: DDXPlus, Sports Understanding, NI Task 280, MultiFin
claims.statement: JSON-mode improves classification task performance by constraining possible answers and reducing errors in answer selection
Removing schema restriction from format-restricting prompts significantly improves average scores on GSM8K across Claude-3-Haiku, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct with lower standard deviations

claims.dataset: Last Letter, GSM8K, DDXPlus, Sports, Task280, MultiFin
claims.statement: Parsing errors are not the primary cause of performance differences between formats despite near-zero parsing failure rates in some models (e.g., Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-3.5-Turbo exhibit near zero parsing failures but still show substantial performance gaps)
NL-to-Format shows nearly identical performance to unrestricted Natural Language responses across most models, with only minor degradation in some cases
GPT-4-turbo as an LLM-based answer parser achieves 97.7% average alignment with human-parsed answers across Last Letter, Shuffle Object, and GSM8K tasks
